text:
Recent Results of Archaeological Investigations at the Former Jewish Cemetery in the New Town, Prague Archaelogical rescue excavations were carried out in the New Town of Prague between 2009 and 2014 in the area known as the “Jewish Garden”, the largest medieval Jewish burial site in Bohemia. The site was in use from mid-13th century to late Middle Ages up to 1478. The recent investigation (carried out due to the Quadrio multifunctional complex construction) modified our knowledge of the Cemetery’s extent in the northern and north-western part: the Cemetery stretched far more to the north than we believed so far.

According to the authors, the investigations revealed a more complex settlement mosaic than had been expected, and that the cemetery was located on top of the remains of an earlier 12th-century settlement layer of the abandoned medieval village of Újezd of St. Martin. The burial density is not uniform, with the highest number of burial found in the central part of the site, while the peripheral area was marked by a dry moat and special structures at the boundaries. In addition to the numerous human remains, the investigation uncovered a remarkable 24 fragments of sandstone tombstones from the second half of the 14th and the first half of the 15th centuries. The presence of unfinished stelae suggests the existence of local stonemasonry workshops producing Jewish funerary monuments. The results of the study are highly significant for understanding the spatial development of the medieval Jewish community of Prague and the character of its burial practices, and serve to underscore the necessity of continuous monitoring of the Prague historical landscape, an extraordinary cultural heritage area.
